algorithms.name: Reverse Linked List
algorithms.analogy: Imagine a chain of train cars. Each car points to the next one. To reverse, you walk the chain and flip each coupling: instead of Car A pointing forward to Car B, Car B now points back to Car A. Three workers (prev, curr, next) walk the chain together — next remembers where to go, curr flips its link, prev records the last flipped car.
algorithms.story: Reversing a linked list is the 'hello world' of pointer manipulation. Master the three-pointer dance: prev=null, curr=head, next=curr.next. At each step: save next, flip curr's link to prev, advance both prev and curr. When curr is null, prev is the new head.
algorithms.whenToUse: Palindrome linked list check, reversing sub-lists (K-group), rotating list, reading list backwards without extra space.
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Initialize prev=null, curr=head
2. While curr != null:
   a. Save next = curr.next
   b. curr.next = prev  (flip the link)
   c. prev = curr       (advance prev)
   d. curr = next       (advance curr)
3. Return prev as new head
algorithms.interviewTips: Draw boxes and arrows before coding. Interviewers love seeing you trace through [1→2→3→null] step by step. Common follow-up: reverse only positions m to n. Edge cases: null list, single node, reversing entire list.
algorithms.useCases: ["Palindrome linked list","K-group reversal","Rotate linked list by K","Check if list is palindrome"]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Forgetting to save next before overwriting curr.next","Off-by-one: returning curr instead of prev at the end","Recursive version — forgetting head.next = null causes cycle"]
algorithms.variants: ["Reverse in K-groups","Reverse between positions m and n","Check palindrome using reversal"]

algorithms.name: Floyd's Cycle Detection
algorithms.analogy: Two runners on a circular track. The tortoise runs 1 step/second, the hare runs 2 steps/second. If there's a loop, the hare will eventually lap the tortoise — they MUST meet inside the cycle. If there's no loop, the hare reaches the finish line (null) first.
algorithms.story: Floyd's algorithm solves 'does this list have a cycle?' in O(1) space. No HashSet needed. The mathematical proof: if a cycle of length C exists and the tortoise enters it at step μ, both pointers are always separated by a multiple of C inside the cycle — they will collide. Phase 2 finds the entry point: reset slow to head, advance both at speed 1, they meet exactly at the cycle start.
algorithms.whenToUse: Detect cycle in linked list, find cycle start, find duplicate in array (array-as-linked-list trick), detect loop in functional sequences.
algorithms.howItWorks:
Phase 1 – Detect:
1. slow = head, fast = head
2. While fast != null && fast.next != null:
   slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next.next
3. If fast == null → no cycle
4. If slow == fast → cycle exists

Phase 2 – Find Entry:
5. Reset slow = head
6. Move both slow and fast ONE step at a time
7. Where they meet = cycle entry node
algorithms.interviewTips: Phase 2 is the hard part — explain WHY resetting slow to head and moving both at speed 1 finds the entry. The math: distance from head to entry = distance from meeting point to entry (within the cycle). This is a common FAANG follow-up.
algorithms.useCases: ["Detect cycle in linked list (LeetCode 141)","Find cycle start (LeetCode 142)","Find duplicate number in array (LeetCode 287)","Happy number problem (LeetCode 202)"]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Checking slow==fast before moving (both start at head, would immediately return true)","Forgetting fast.next != null check (NPE on fast.next.next)","Phase 2: forgetting to move BOTH pointers one step at a time"]
algorithms.variants: ["Find cycle length","Find duplicate number (array as linked list)","Happy number detection"]

algorithms.name: Merge Two Sorted Lists
algorithms.analogy: Two sorted card decks face-up on the table. You compare the top card of each deck, pick the smaller one into the result pile, and repeat. When one deck is empty, flip the remaining deck onto the pile. The dummy head trick is like having a placeholder card at the start so you never need special handling for the first pick.
algorithms.story: The dummy head pattern is the key insight here. Create a dummy node before the real list starts — the merged.next of the last appended node becomes your current pointer. Compare l1.val vs l2.val, attach the smaller node, advance that list's pointer. At the end, attach whichever list still has nodes. Return dummy.next.
algorithms.whenToUse: Merge K sorted lists (divide and conquer), merge sort on linked lists, combining query results, streaming merge of sorted data.
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Create dummy = new ListNode(0), curr = dummy
2. While l1 != null AND l2 != null:
   - If l1.val <= l2.val: curr.next = l1; l1 = l1.next
   - Else: curr.next = l2; l2 = l2.next
   - curr = curr.next
3. Attach remaining: curr.next = (l1 != null) ? l1 : l2
4. Return dummy.next
algorithms.interviewTips: The dummy head node pattern eliminates special-casing the first node — use it any time you're building a new linked list. Follow-up: Merge K sorted lists using a min-heap of size K (O(n log K)) or recursive divide-and-conquer.
algorithms.useCases: ["Merge K sorted lists","Merge sort on linked lists","Combine sorted streams","LeetCode 21"]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Forgetting curr.next = l1 != null ? l1 : l2 to attach the remaining list","Moving curr before setting curr.next","Modifying l1/l2 pointers without saving next"]
algorithms.variants: ["Merge K sorted lists (min-heap)","Sort linked list (merge sort)","Merge sorted arrays"]

algorithms.name: Find Middle Node
algorithms.analogy: One person walks, another runs double speed. When the runner reaches the end, the walker is exactly in the middle. This is the fast/slow pointer trick — the fast pointer covers 2 nodes per step, so when it hits null (or the last node), the slow pointer is at the midpoint.
algorithms.story: The fast/slow pointer approach finds the middle in one pass without knowing the length. For even-length lists, there are two valid midpoints — fast != null gives the first middle, fast.next != null gives the second. This pattern is the backbone of linked list merge sort and palindrome checking.
algorithms.whenToUse: Merge sort on linked lists, palindrome check (split + reverse second half), finding median in a stream, reorder list problem.
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. slow = head, fast = head
2. While fast != null AND fast.next != null:
   slow = slow.next
   fast = fast.next.next
3. slow is now the middle node

For even lists:
- fast != null condition → first of two middles
- fast.next != null condition → second of two middles
algorithms.interviewTips: Clarify what 'middle' means for even-length lists before coding. Combine with reversal for palindrome check — a classic two-algorithm chaining problem. For merge sort, split at the node BEFORE the middle (use fast.next.next != null and break one step earlier).
algorithms.useCases: ["Palindrome linked list","Merge sort on linked list","Reorder list (LeetCode 143)","Delete middle node"]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Off-by-one for even lists — first vs second middle","Not understanding which condition to use for which problem","For merge sort, need to split the list (set slow.next = null)"]
algorithms.variants: ["Palindrome linked list","Reorder list","Delete middle node","Linked list merge sort"]

algorithms.name: Remove Nth Node From End
algorithms.analogy: Two runners start together. Runner A gets a head start of N steps. Then both run at the same speed. When Runner A reaches the end of the track, Runner B is exactly N steps behind — pointing at the node to delete. The dummy head trick handles the edge case of deleting the first node.
algorithms.story: The N-gap two-pointer technique eliminates the need for a second pass. Advance the fast pointer N+1 steps ahead (using a dummy head makes this clean). Then advance both until fast is null. At that point, slow.next is the target — set slow.next = slow.next.next to remove it.
algorithms.whenToUse: Any 'Kth from end' problem: find Kth from end, remove Kth from end, split list at Kth from end.
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Create dummy node, dummy.next = head
2. slow = dummy, fast = dummy
3. Advance fast N+1 steps (not N, because slow starts at dummy)
4. While fast != null:
   slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next
5. slow.next = slow.next.next  (delete the Nth from end)
6. Return dummy.next
algorithms.interviewTips: Always use a dummy head when you might delete the first node. The N+1 advance (not N) is because slow starts at dummy (one step before head). Trace through [1→2→3→4→5], n=2 to verify: fast goes to node 3 after 3 advances, then both walk until fast=null, slow ends at node 3, deletes node 4.
algorithms.useCases: ["Remove Nth node from end (LeetCode 19)","Find Kth from end","Split list at midpoint","Delete last occurrence"]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Advancing fast N steps instead of N+1 (slow ends up at the node to delete, not the predecessor)","Not using dummy head — fails when deleting the first node","Off-by-one in the advance loop"]
algorithms.variants: ["Find Kth node from end","Split list into two halves","Remove last occurrence of value"]
